quick_facts.label: What it is
quick_facts.value: The National Apprenticeship Certificate (NAC) is awarded to a trade apprentice who passes the All India Trade Test (AITT) for Apprentices under the Apprentices Act, 1961; it is not issued merely for finishing the workplace-training period.

quick_facts.label: Who it is for
quick_facts.value: It applies to trade apprentices in designated trades. ITI/NTC holders may enter related trade apprenticeships, but freshers may also be engaged where they meet the trade’s prescribed entry requirements.

quick_facts.label: Training length
quick_facts.value: NAC-linked trade-apprenticeship duration is trade- and entry-route-specific, rather than universally one year. DGT’s current FAQ describes a range of 6 months to 4 years; the NAPS page says designated-trade training for fresher/non-ITI candidates ordinarily runs two years.

quick_facts.label: Recognition
quick_facts.value: DGT states that NAC is recognized for employment under Government and semi-Government departments and organisations, and describes it as a recognized qualification for seeking employment.

quick_facts.label: Training setting
quick_facts.value: Trade apprenticeship combines workplace practical training with basic training and related instruction under the prescribed syllabus for the designated trade.

sections.heading: Eligibility and route from ITI
paragraphs: A candidate must be at least 14 years old and meet the physical and educational standards applicable under the Apprentices Act, 1961. For designated trades connected with hazardous industries, the stated minimum age is 18 years.

An ITI pass-out can seek engagement as a trade apprentice in a related trade. However, an ITI is not an absolute prerequisite for every NAC route: DGT states that freshers/non-ITI candidates may also undergo trade apprenticeship, subject to the requirements of the particular trade and establishment.

NAC eligibility comes at the end of the training route: after successful completion of apprenticeship training in an establishment, the trade apprentice is eligible to appear in the AITT for Apprentices; passing that test leads to the NAC.
bullets: Check the educational qualification and medical/physical requirements specified for the individual designated trade.
Ensure the apprenticeship is a trade-apprentice engagement in a designated trade if the intended outcome is the AITT-for-Apprentices/NAC route.
An ITI National Trade Certificate (NTC) can be a relevant entry qualification for a related apprenticeship, but the exact entry pathway varies by trade.

sections.heading: Duration: not inherently a one-year certification
paragraphs: The duration belongs to the apprenticeship programme, not to the certificate itself. DGT’s FAQ says trade-apprenticeship duration varies from 6 months to 4 years, while its apprenticeship-training overview describes trade-apprentice periods as varying by trade and entry route.

For fresher/non-ITI candidates in designated trades, the National Apprenticeship Promotion Scheme (NAPS) information says the duration ordinarily is two years. Consequently, describing NAC as a universal one-year certification is inaccurate; candidates should verify the notified syllabus and training period for their exact trade.

One year is more specifically associated in official material with graduate, technician and technician (vocational) apprenticeship categories, whose completion credential is described separately from the trade-apprentice NAC route.
bullets: Confirm the trade-specific period before accepting an offer.
Do not confuse a one-year graduate/diploma apprenticeship with the trade-apprentice pathway that culminates in NAC.
Allow time after training for the applicable AITT process and certificate issuance.

sections.heading: What is learned and assessed
paragraphs: The content is trade-specific. DGT lists designated trades and publishes their related details/syllabi; examples on the official list include Electrician, Fitter, Machinist, Draughtsman (Mechanical), CNC Programmer-cum-Operator and many others.

DGT describes trade-apprenticeship training as basic training and practical training followed by related instruction, delivered in line with the prescribed syllabus. This makes the NAC route substantially workplace-based while retaining structured trade knowledge and instruction.

The certification assessment is the All India Trade Test for Apprentices. DGT states that AITT examinations for ATS use computer-based theory examinations, while practical examinations are conducted by the respective establishments/institutes.
bullets: Basic training: foundational trade skills for candidates who need it.
Practical/on-the-job training: work performed in the employer’s establishment.
Related instruction and AITT assessment: trade theory plus practical evaluation under the apprenticeship testing system.

sections.heading: How to enter the NAC pathway
paragraphs: There is no standalone, central 'NAC course admission' in the way there is an ITI admission. The practical route is to obtain engagement as a trade apprentice with an eligible establishment in an appropriate designated trade, then enter into a contract of apprenticeship with that employer.

DGT states that the apprenticeship contract between apprentice and employer is registered by Apprenticeship Advisers. Its FAQ further explains that candidates enter a contract with establishments, training begins when the contract is signed, and the contracts are verified by the relevant Regional Directorates/authorities.

Applicants should use the official apprenticeship system and employer/establishment vacancies to identify openings, then check the employer’s trade, qualification, age, medical and document requirements. Selection processes and availability are establishment- and trade-dependent.
bullets: Identify a designated trade aligned with prior ITI training or other qualifying education.
Apply for an apprenticeship engagement with an establishment.
Complete the apprenticeship contract and required verification/registration steps.
Finish the prescribed training and take the AITT for Apprentices to qualify for NAC.

sections.heading: Employment and further-training options
paragraphs: NAC is a recognized employment qualification for relevant roles in Government/semi-Government organisations and industry, according to DGT. Actual recruitment eligibility remains controlled by the employer’s recruitment notice, including the specific trade, experience, age, medical and reservation requirements.

For teaching/training progression, NAC can be an accepted entry qualification for certain Craft Instructor Training Scheme (CITS) courses where the course-specific eligibility explicitly lists a National Apprenticeship Certificate in the related trade. This is not a blanket rule for all CITS trades, so applicants must read the specific course entry criteria.

The certificate is especially useful as evidence of completion of structured, assessed apprenticeship training in the named trade; it should be paired with the trade’s syllabus, experience record and the requirements in any target employer or examination notification.
bullets: Apply to trade-relevant industrial, technical and public-sector vacancies when the recruitment notice accepts the qualification.
Consider a related CITS programme only where that programme’s official eligibility includes NAC or another qualification you hold.
Verify every next-step examination or employer notice independently; NAC recognition does not automatically satisfy all post-specific conditions.
